lessons.meaning: as soon as (one thing happens, the next follows)
lessons.example_en: As soon as I arrived home, I went to sleep.
lessons.note: Attach **-자마자** directly to a verb stem (no tense marker) to show the second action happens right after the first.

sentences.english: As soon as I woke up, I drank water.
As soon as class ended, I went home.
As soon as I heard the news, I called my friend.
As soon as I sat down, the phone rang.
As soon as I get home, I will rest.

lessons.example_en: While I was studying, my younger sibling slept.
lessons.note: Use **-는 동안(에)** after a verb stem for "while doing," or attach **동안** to a noun (방학 동안 = during the vacation).

sentences.english: While I was sleeping, it rained.
Please wait while I cook.
While my mother was out, I cleaned the house.
accepted: 어머니가 안 계시는 동안 집을 청소했어요.
어머니가 안 계시는 동안에 집을 청소했어요.

sentences.english: I read a book while waiting for the bus.
While I was studying abroad, I learned a lot.

lessons.meaning: in order to / for the purpose of
lessons.example_en: I exercise in order to become healthy.
lessons.note: Attach **-기 위해(서)** to a verb stem for a goal, or use **N을/를 위해(서)** with a noun (가족을 위해서 = for my family).

sentences.english: I study hard in order to pass the exam.
I save money in order to travel.
I get up early in order to catch the train.
I came to Korea in order to learn Korean.
I work hard for my family.

lessons.meaning: reported speech: to say / said that ~
lessons.example_en: My friend said (that) he will come tomorrow.
lessons.note: Verbs take **-ㄴ다고/는다고 하다** (간다고, 먹는다고), adjectives **-다고** (예쁘다고), and nouns **-(이)라고** (학생이라고).

sentences.english: My friend says he goes to school.
My older brother said he eats breakfast every day.
She says the flowers are pretty.
He said he is a student.
The teacher said tomorrow there is no class.

lessons.meaning: because of (doing something, with a negative or hindered result)
lessons.example_en: I couldn't sleep last night because I was studying.
lessons.note: **-느라고** links a cause to a negative or failed result; both clauses share the same subject and the cause is an action verb, not an adjective.

sentences.english: I was busy working, so I couldn't call you.
I overslept, so I was late.
I was watching a movie, so I didn't hear the phone.
sentences.korean: 영화를 보느라고 전화 소리를 못 들었어요.
accepted: 영화를 보느라고 전화 소리를 못 들었어요.
영화를 보느라고 전화 소리를 못 들었습니다.
영화를 보느라 전화 소리를 못 들었어요.

sentences.english: I was preparing for the exam, so I was very tired.
I was playing games, so I forgot my homework.

lessons.example_en: Even though I studied hard, I did poorly on the exam.
lessons.note: Use **-는데도** with verbs in the present, **-았/었는데도** for the past, and **-(으)ㄴ데도** with adjectives (바쁜데도 = even though busy).

sentences.english: Even though it is raining, I am going out.
Even though I ate a lot, I am still hungry.
Even though it is expensive, a lot of people buy it.
Even though he is busy, he always helps me.
Even though I called many times, he didn't answer.

lessons.note: **-(으)ㄹ 뻔하다** describes something that nearly happened; it is almost always used in the past tense as **-(으)ㄹ 뻔했어요**.

sentences.english: I almost missed the bus.
I almost forgot your name.
I was so tired I almost fell asleep.
I almost fell because of the ice.
I was so surprised I almost cried.

lessons.meaning: do something in advance and leave it that way
lessons.example_en: I bought the tickets in advance (and kept them ready).
lessons.note: Attach **-아/어 두다** or **-아/어 놓다** to a verb to show you finished an action and left the result in place for later.

sentences.english: I made the food in advance.
Please leave the window open.
I wrote down the address beforehand.
I reserved a seat in advance.
Keep the door closed, please.
